name: processes
context: Launch, monitor, signal, background, and terminate processes from the shell
info: Process management is essential for agents that run services, manage long-running tasks, or need to inspect system state. This skill covers the full process lifecycle: launching processes (foreground, background, nohup), monitoring them (ps, top, htop, pgrep), sending signals (kill, pkill, trap), managing job control (jobs, bg, fg, disown, wait), inspecting process relationships (pstree, /proc), and checking resource usage (lsof, /proc/PID/status). Agents use this skill to start development servers, manage background workers, detect hanging processes, clean up after failed commands, and coordinate parallel execution. See [[shell/bash]] for command execution, [[shell/environment]] for process environment inheritance, and [[shell/pipes]] for inter-process communication via pipes.
triggers: run a process in the background
find which process is using a port
check if a process is running
send a signal to a process
wait for a process to finish
detach a process from the terminal
body:
# Process Management

Processes are running instances of programs. Every command an agent runs spawns a process with a PID, environment, open file descriptors, and a parent-child relationship.

## Launching Processes

```bash
# Foreground (blocks until complete)
command arg1 arg2

# Background (returns immediately, shell gets PID)
command arg1 arg2 &
bg_pid=$!                        # PID of last background process

# Background with output suppressed
command > /dev/null 2>&1 &

# Survive terminal close (detach from session)
nohup command > output.log 2>&1 &

# Replace current shell with process
exec python app.py               # PID stays the same

# Run with modified environment (single command)
env PORT=8080 DEBUG=true command
PORT=8080 DEBUG=true command     # Bash shorthand

# Run with timeout
timeout 30 long_running_command  # Kill after 30 seconds
timeout -s SIGKILL 30 command    # Force kill after 30s
```

## Listing and Finding Processes

```bash
# List all processes (full format)
ps aux                           # BSD-style: all users, full
ps -ef                           # POSIX-style: every process, full

# Find process by name
ps aux | grep '[n]ode'           # Brackets trick excludes grep itself
pgrep -f 'node server'          # Pattern match on full command line
pgrep -la node                   # List PIDs and command names

# Process tree
pstree -p                        # Show hierarchy with PIDs
pstree -p $(pgrep -f 'node')    # Tree rooted at specific process

# Interactive process monitor
top                              # Real-time process viewer
htop                             # Improved interactive viewer (if installed)

# Find process using a port
lsof -i :3000                    # What's listening on port 3000
fuser 3000/tcp                   # PIDs using port 3000
ss -tlnp | grep :3000            # Socket statistics
netstat -tlnp | grep :3000       # Legacy equivalent

# Find process using a file
lsof /path/to/file               # Processes with file open
fuser /path/to/file              # PIDs using file
```

## Signals

Signals are how the kernel and processes communicate. Key signals:

| Signal | Number | Default | Purpose |
|--------|--------|---------|---------|
| `SIGHUP` | 1 | Terminate | Terminal hangup — often used to reload config |
| `SIGINT` | 2 | Terminate | Interrupt (Ctrl-C) |
| `SIGQUIT` | 3 | Core dump | Quit with core dump (Ctrl-\\) |
| `SIGKILL` | 9 | Terminate | Force kill — cannot be caught or ignored |
| `SIGTERM` | 15 | Terminate | Graceful termination (default for `kill`) |
| `SIGSTOP` | 19 | Stop | Pause process — cannot be caught |
| `SIGCONT` | 18 | Continue | Resume stopped process |
| `SIGUSR1` | 10 | Terminate | User-defined signal 1 |
| `SIGUSR2` | 12 | Terminate | User-defined signal 2 |

```bash
# Send signals
kill PID                          # SIGTERM (graceful)
kill -9 PID                       # SIGKILL (force)
kill -HUP PID                     # SIGHUP (reload config)
kill -STOP PID                    # Pause process
kill -CONT PID                    # Resume process

# Kill by name
pkill node                         # SIGTERM to all 'node' processes
pkill -9 -f 'node server.js'      # SIGKILL matching full command
killall python                     # SIGTERM to all 'python' processes

# Kill process group (all children)
kill -- -PGID                      # Negative PID = process group
kill -TERM -- -$(ps -o pgid= -p PID | tr -d ' ')  # Kill group of PID
```

## Job Control

```bash
# Start background job
command &

# List jobs in current shell
jobs                    # Show all jobs
jobs -l                 # Include PIDs

# Suspend foreground process
# Press Ctrl-Z (sends SIGTSTP)

# Resume in background/foreground
bg %1                   # Resume job 1 in background
fg %1                   # Bring job 1 to foreground

# Detach job from shell (survives shell exit)
disown %1               # Detach job 1
disown -h %1            # Detach but keep in job table

# Wait for background jobs
wait                    # Wait for ALL background jobs
wait $pid               # Wait for specific PID
wait -n                 # Wait for ANY single job (Bash 4.3+)
```

## Process Information (Linux /proc)

```bash
# Process status
cat /proc/PID/status              # Name, state, memory, threads
cat /proc/PID/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '  # Full command line
cat /proc/PID/environ | tr '\0' '\n'  # Environment variables
ls -l /proc/PID/fd                # Open file descriptors
cat /proc/PID/limits              # Resource limits

# System-wide
cat /proc/loadavg                 # Load averages
cat /proc/meminfo                 # Memory statistics
cat /proc/cpuinfo                 # CPU information
uptime                            # Load averages + uptime
free -h                           # Memory usage summary
```

## Common Agent Patterns

```bash
# Start server, wait for ready, then continue
node server.js &
server_pid=$!

# Wait for port to be open
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  if ss -tlnp | grep -q ':3000'; then
    echo "Server ready"
    break
  fi
  sleep 1
done

# Cleanup on exit
trap "kill $server_pid 2>/dev/null" EXIT

# Run with timeout and cleanup
timeout 60 long_command || {
  echo "Command timed out" >&2
  exit 1
}

# Parallel execution with wait
command1 &
command2 &
command3 &
wait  # Wait for all to finish

# Check if port is available before starting
if ss -tlnp | grep -q ':3000'; then
  echo "Port 3000 already in use" >&2
  lsof -i :3000
  exit 1
fi
```

sections.title: Signals and kill
sections.body: Signals are asynchronous notifications sent to processes. `kill PID` sends SIGTERM (15) — the process can catch it and shut down gracefully. `kill -9 PID` sends SIGKILL — cannot be caught, kernel terminates immediately. `pkill` and `killall` kill by name/pattern. `trap 'cleanup' SIGTERM` in scripts catches signals for graceful shutdown. Always try SIGTERM first, wait a few seconds, then SIGKILL if still alive. Signal 0 (`kill -0 PID`) checks if process exists without sending a signal.

sections.title: Job control
sections.body: Job control manages processes within a shell session. `command &` starts in background. `Ctrl-Z` suspends foreground. `bg` resumes suspended job in background. `fg` brings job to foreground. `jobs -l` lists jobs with PIDs. `disown` detaches from shell (survives logout). `wait` blocks until background jobs finish — `wait $pid` for specific PID, `wait -n` for any one job (Bash 4.3+). `$!` holds PID of last background process. `nohup` prevents SIGHUP on terminal close.

sections.title: Finding and inspecting processes
sections.body: `ps aux` lists all processes (BSD style). `ps -ef` is the POSIX equivalent. `pgrep -f pattern` finds PIDs matching a pattern in the full command line. `pstree -p` shows the process hierarchy. `lsof -i :PORT` finds what's listening on a port. `fuser PORT/tcp` does the same. `ss -tlnp` shows all listening TCP sockets. On Linux, `/proc/PID/status` has detailed process info: state, memory, threads, capabilities. `/proc/PID/fd/` shows open file descriptors.

sections.title: Port and network process management
sections.body: Agents frequently need to start servers and manage ports. Check if a port is in use: `ss -tlnp | grep :PORT` or `lsof -i :PORT`. Find the PID: `fuser PORT/tcp` or parse `lsof` output. Kill the process on a port: `fuser -k PORT/tcp`. Wait for a port to become available: poll with `ss` or use `nc -z localhost PORT` in a retry loop. Common ports: 3000 (React dev), 5173 (Vite), 8080 (generic), 5432 (PostgreSQL), 6379 (Redis), 27017 (MongoDB).
